chapter.p: Vance continued to sit there. He had imagined he had suffered on the day when he had seen his grandfather down by the river with Floss Delaney: poor simpleton! That was a wound to his raw senses. He had escaped from it by writing it out and selling it to an editor. But now there was not a vein of his body, not a cell of his brain, not a dream or a vision of his soul, that was not hurt, disabled.вБ† вБ†вА¶ This woman who had kindled in him the light by which he lived had sat there complacently telling him that she believed in his work, that her husband and old Frenside believed in itвБ† вБ†вА¶ and had thought she was leaving him comforted!

But did she really think so? Or was all she had said only a protective disguise, the conscientious effort to repress emotions corresponding to his own? He had an idea she would be very conscientious, full of scruples he wasnвАЩt sure he wholly understood. For if she hadnвАЩt cared as much as he did, why should she have devoted all those hours to helping him? If it was just for the good of the New Hour, she was indeed the ideal wife for an editor! But no: those afternoons had been as full for her as for him. What was that phrase she had pointed out, in the volume of KeatsвАЩs Letters she had given himвБ†вАФabout loading every rift with gold? That was what they had done to their hours together: both of them.

Suddenly, as he sat brooding, he heard a door open: then, after a momentвАЩs delay, a step coming through the empty rooms. The carpets muffled it, the stealing twilight seemed to envelop it; but it was hers, hers surelyвБ†вАФwho else would have business there? She was returning, coming back to say all the things that were surging in his heart.вБ† вБ†вА¶ He sat still, not daring to look up.

The step drew nearer, reached the threshold, clicked on the parquet of the library. He started up and saw Mrs. Tracy. In the faint light her face looked so drawn and wretched that he thought she had been taken ill again, and went toward her hurriedly.
вАЬWhy, whatвАЩs the matter?вАЭ

вАЬMatter? It is a woman you come to meet here then!вАЭ She had reached the table, and with a quick pounce picked up a glove which lay there. вАЬThatвАЩs what you call your literary work, is it?вАЭ she triumphed venomously.

Vance stood silent. His mind was still so charged with ardent and agonizing thoughts that he could not grasp what she was saying. What was she talking about, what was she trying to insinuate, what had she come for? Once more he caught in her face the gleam of animosity he had been conscious of, just below the surface, ever since he had gone with Upton to the ball game.
вАЬWell, havenвАЩt you got anything to say? No, I donвАЩt suppose you have!вАЭ Mrs. Tracy taunted him.
вАЬI donвАЩt know what you expect me to say. I donвАЩt know what youвАЩre talking about. That glove is Mrs. TarrantвАЩsвБ†вАФshe left here only a few minutes ago.вАЭ
Mrs. TracyвАЩs sallow face grew sallower. He saw that she was unprepared for the answer and not wholly inclined to believe it. вАЬMrs. TarrantвБ†вАФwhat was she doing here?вАЭ
вАЬShe came to see me.вАЭ
вАЬAnd what were you doing here?вАЭ
вАЬWriting, as you see.вАЭ
Mrs. Tracy was silent for a moment, her eyes fixed incredulously on the piled-up pages before her. вАЬIвАЩd like to know who it is lets you in,вАЭ she said at length.
вАЬWhy, Mrs. Tarrant let me in today, of course.вАЭ
вАЬToday! Maybe she did. IвАЩm not talking only about today. ItвАЩs not the only day youвАЩve been here.вАЭ

Vance hesitated. He had expected to silence his mother-in-law, and dispel her suspicions, by naming Mrs. TarrantвБ†вАФone of the few persons who had the undisputed right to come and go in that house. But it would be a different matter, he instantly felt, to let Mrs. Tracy associate HaloвАЩs name with the frequent and clandestine visits of which she evidently suspected him. He was convinced now that she had come on purpose to surprise him, as the result of information received; and he was never ready-witted in emergencies.
вАЬWell, I donвАЩt knowвАЩs I need ask who lets you in,вАЭ she pursued. вАЬYou had plenty of time to have duplicate keys made while I was sick.вАЭ
вАЬCertainly I hadвБ†вАФif it had occurred to me to do anything so low-down.вАЭ

вАЬLow-down? I guess it isnвАЩt that would have prevented you, if youвАЩd been set on coming here, whether it was to steal books or to meet womenвБ† вБ†вА¶ maybe bothвБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ she flung back, trembling.

Her agitation had a steadying effect on Vance. вАЬWhy not both, as you say?вАЭ he rejoined impartially, beginning to gather up his papers. He was sure she was not there without a definite purpose, and it was obviously safer to leave the burden of explanation on her shoulders. After all, he had nothing to reproach himself with but the venial wrong of concealing from Laura Lou that he did his writing at the Willows, and not at the New Hour office. He had been slaving all summer to pay off the money Mrs. Tracy had accepted from Bunty Hayes, and the women had better leave him alone, or heвАЩd know why.вБ† вБ†вА¶ Silently he crammed his papers into their usual storing-place and walked toward the door.
Mrs. Tracy stepped in front of him. вАЬWhere are you going?вАЭ
вАЬHome.вАЭ
вАЬHome? ItвАЩs a place you donвАЩt often trouble. Why donвАЩt you do your writing thereвБ†вАФif itвАЩs writing you say you come here for?вАЭ
вАЬBecause IвАЩm never left alone,вАЭ he said, his anger rising again. Mrs. Tracy saw her chance and laughed. вАЬNot with the right woman, you mean?вАЭ

Vance halted in front of her. After all, if there was a scene comingвБ†вАФand he saw she was not to be cheated out of itвБ†вАФbetter have it out here than wait and risk Laura LouвАЩs being drawn in. вАЬWhat is it youвАЩre driving at? I canвАЩt answer till I know,вАЭ he said sullenly.
вАЬWell, answer me this, then. WhoвАЩs the woman you come here to meet?вАЭ
The blood rose to his face. вАЬNonsense. I told you Mrs. Tarrant came here today. YouвАЩd better give me her glove and IвАЩll take it back to her.вАЭ

Mrs. Tracy paid no attention to this. She hesitated a moment; then she said: вАЬYou havenвАЩt answered my question yet. ItвАЩs no good beating round the bush. The neighbours all know about whatвАЩs been going on here. Laura LouвАЩs had a letter warning her. You say, what am I driving at? Well, IвАЩm here to find out what you propose to do, now weвАЩve caught you. ThatвАЩs plain enough, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ She flung the words out in a kind of shrill monotone, as if she had learned them from someone else and were afraid of not getting them in the right order.

Vance was speechless. His mind had seized on one phrase: вАЬLaura LouвАЩs had a letter,вАЭ and he turned sick with an unformed apprehension. вАЬWhat nonsense are you talking? What kind of letter? IвАЩve got nothing to hide and nothing to explain. If you have the letter with you, youвАЩd better let me see it, and if I can find the sneak who wrote it IвАЩll go and break his neck.вАЭ
Mrs. Tracy laughed. вАЬWell, youвАЩll have some trouble doing that, I guess. But the letter isnвАЩt hereвБ†вАФitвАЩs locked up at home. ItвАЩs done enough harm to my poor child alreadyвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬWho wrote it?вАЭ Vance interrupted.
вАЬItвАЩs not signed.вАЭ

вАЬI thought as much. That kind never is. And youвАЩve come here to spy on me on the strength of a rag of paper with God knows what anonymous slander on it?вАЭ He took his hat up again, and as he did so, his eye lit on the keys which Halo, in leaving, had laid beside him. They were no good to him now; he would never use them again, never come back here without her. He would take them back to Eaglewood this very night, with the glove.вБ† вБ†вА¶

He put them in his pocket and turned again to his mother-in-law. вАЬI come here to work, not to meet women.вАЭ This sounded impressive, and in a way was trueвБ†вАФyet he didnвАЩt care for the ring of it. He cleared his throat, and began again: вАЬIf youвАЩll give me Mrs. TarrantвАЩs glove IвАЩll send it back.вАЭ

For all answer Mrs. Tracy opened her handbag (it was the very one the bridal couple had bought for her on their return to PaulвАЩs Landing), and put the glove in it, snapping shut the imitation ivory clasp.
вАЬSee hereвБ†вАФgive me that glove,вАЭ Vance burst out; then crimsoned at his blunder. Mrs. Tracy tucked the bag under her arm. вАЬIвАЩll see to returning it,вАЭ she said.
He affected indifference. вАЬOh, very wellвБ†вАФвАЭ There was a pause, and then he added. вАЬIf thatвАЩs all, IвАЩll be off.вАЭ
Mrs. Tracy, however, continued to oppose him from the threshold. вАЬItвАЩs not allвБ†вАФnothing like. I guess itвАЩs for me to decide about that.вАЭ

Vance waited a moment: angry as he was, he had the sense to want to check Mrs. TracyвАЩs recriminations. вАЬIf thereвАЩs anything else to be said, I guess itвАЩs for Laura Lou to say it. IвАЩm going back home now to give her the chance,вАЭ he declared.

Mrs. Tracy raised her hand in agitation. вАЬNo, VanceвБ†вАФno! You wonвАЩt do that. The letterвАЩs half killed her, anyway. And you know she canвАЩt stand anything that excites and worries her.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ She paused a moment, and added with a certain dignity: вАЬThatвАЩs why I came hereвБ†вАФit was to spare her.вАЭ
Vance pondered. вАЬWas it her idea that you should come?вАЭ

вАЬNo, she doesnвАЩt even know IвАЩm here. I told her weвАЩd have to take steps to find outвБ†вАФbut she was so upset she wouldnвАЩt listen. My childвАЩs a nervous wreck, Vance. ThatвАЩs what youвАЩve made of her.вАЭ
вАЬIf IвАЩve made her a nervous wreck, is it your idea that itвАЩs going to quiet her if you go back and reportвБ†вАФas you apparently mean to doвБ†вАФthat I come here to meet other women?вАЭ
The reasoning of this was a little too close for Mrs. TracyвАЩs flurried brain. She considered it for a while, and then said: вАЬI didnвАЩt come here to go back and report to her.вАЭ

Vance looked at her in astonishment. вАЬThen what on earth did you come for? Your imagination is so worked up against me that all my denials wouldnвАЩt convince youвБ†вАФI see that. But if Laura LouвАЩs to be left out of the questionвБ†вАФвАЭ
His mother-in-law moved nearer to him, with a look of appeal in her face that made it human again. вАЬIt all depends on you, Vance.вАЭ
вАЬWell, you donвАЩt supposeвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt suppose you want to hurt Laura Lou more than you can helpвБ†вАФany more than I do,вАЭ she continued, with an effort at persuasiveness. вАЬAnd what IвАЩm here for is to ask you to spare herвБ† вБ†вА¶ give her a chanceвБ† вБ†вА¶ before itвАЩs too late.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ She lifted her hands entreatingly. вАЬFor GodвАЩs sake, Vance, let her go without a fight. ItвАЩs her chance now, and I mean she shall take it; but if youвАЩll let it be easy for her, IвАЩll let it be easy for youвБ†вАФon my sacred word. VanceвБ† вБ†вА¶ See here; IвАЩve got you where I can make my own terms with youвБ† вБ†вА¶ IвАЩve got my proofsвБ† вБ†вА¶ IвАЩve got the whip hand of as they sayвБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ She broke off, and went on in an altered voice: вАЬBut thatвАЩs not the way I want to talk to you, Vance. I just want to say: Why not recognize itвАЩs all been a failure and a mistake from the first, and set my child free before itвАЩs too late?вАЭ
вАЬToo late for what?вАЭ
вАЬFor her to get back her healthвБ†вАФto enjoy her youth as she ought to.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ

VanceвАЩs brain was still so confused with the shock of Mrs. TarrantвАЩs abrupt leave-taking that this fresh assault on his emotions left him dazed. Mrs. Tracy hated him; had always hated him. He had long been carelessly aware of that, and had instantly seen how eagerly she must have caught at this chance of getting the whip hand of him, as she called it; of finding herself justified in all her disappointments and resentments. But he had not suspected that she might have a more practical object in mind, that what she wanted was not to injure him but to free Laura Lou. It was dull of him, no doubt, it was incomprehensible even, that, having lived all his life in a world of painless divorce, where a change of mate was often a mere step in social advancement, it should never have occurred to him that he and Laura Lou could part. But though he had often chafed at the bondage of his unconsidered marriage, though he had long since ceased to think of his wife as the companion of his inner life, and had stooped to subterfuges to escape her fond solicitudeвБ† вБ†вА¶ yet now her motherвАЩs proposal filled him with speechless wrath. He and Laura Lou divorced!вБ† вБ†вА¶ He turned to Mrs. Tracy. вАЬAre you talking seriously?вАЭ
Of course she was, she said. WouldnвАЩt he try and understand her and listen to her, and not get all worked up, and make her so nervous she couldnвАЩt get out what she had to sayвБ† вБ†вА¶ ?
вАЬHave to say?вАЭ he interrupted. вАЬWho obliges you? You say Laura Lou doesnвАЩt know youвАЩre here.вАЭ

Mrs. TracyвАЩs embarrassment increased. When Vance flew out at her like that, she said, she couldnвАЩt keep her wits together; and what was to be gained by making a fuss, anyhow? She was determined, whatever he said, that her child should be free to make a fresh start, and get back her health and spirits.вБ† вБ†вА¶ She talked on and on in the same half scared yet obstinate tone. TheyвАЩd been married too young, she said; that had always been her chief objection to the match. And Vance with no fixed prospectsвБ† вБ†вА¶ or not enough to support a wife on, anyhowвБ† вБ†вА¶ and his parents doing nothingвБ† вБ†вА¶ and Laura LouвАЩs health so delicate, and her lungs threatened since that crazy climb up the mountain in the snow, and no way, as far as her mother knew, for her to get to a mild climate for the winter, as the doctor said she ought to, if she was to get her lungs healed.вБ† вБ†вА¶ Mrs. Tracy paused, breathless and drawn. вАЬIt all depends on you, Vance,вАЭ she began again, still panting a little. вАЬYouвАЩve got your own lifeвБ†вАФyour own ambitions. I donвАЩt say but what youвАЩll go a great way yet, and maybe get onto a big newspaper job some day. But meantime, how are you going to get ahead with a sick wife on your handsвБ†вАФand your own family not doing anything to help?вБ† вБ†вА¶ Let her go, Vance; I say it in your own interest as well as hers; for pityвАЩs sake, let her go. Think what it would be to you to be free yourself.вАЭ
He stood with bent head, her last words resounding strangely in his ears. вАЬThink what it would be to be free yourself.вАЭ He had not thought of that.
вАЬLet her goвБ†вАФgo where?вАЭ

South, Mrs. Tracy hurriedly explainedвБ†вАФto California somewhere. That was what the doctor said. SheвАЩd worked it all out with Upton. Upton had had the offer of a job in a big California nursery, and he would make up his mind to take it if his mother would go out and keep house for him; and Mrs. Tracy would sell the little place at PaulвАЩs Landing for whatever it would bring, if only they could get Laura Lou away before the cold weather.вБ† вБ†вА¶ And out there, in some of those western states, folks said you could get a divorce as easy as you get a cake of yeast at the grocerвАЩs. Just walk in and ask for it; and nothing said against either partyвБ† вБ†вА¶ no question about other women or anythingвБ† вБ†вА¶ so that if Vance wanted to marry again heвАЩd have a clean slateвБ† вБ†вА¶ absolutely.вБ† вБ†вА¶ вАЬOh, Vance, if you only wouldвБ†вАФif youвАЩd only agree to part as friends, and let my child have her chance!вАЭ Mrs. Tracy broke off in a spasm of dry sobbing.

Vance did not afterward recall how the discussion ended. He only knew that dusk had fallen when he and Mrs. Tracy at length left the house. As they turned down the lane they saw the lights of the high road, and the illuminated trolleys jogging by. Mrs. Tracy got into one and disappeared.

Vance sat down under a road lamp and pulled a scrap of paper from his pocket. He wrote on it: вАЬI donвАЩt want these; IвАЩll never go back there,вАЭ wrapped the keys in the paper, and addressed it to Mrs. Tarrant. Then he caught the next trolley, and was carried as far as it went in the direction of Eaglewood. He meant to walk up there and leave the keys for her; after that he would see. The late August night was hushed but not oppressive; the stars already seemed to hang higher than in midsummer, as if receding to autumn altitudes; as he climbed there was a stir of air in the upper branches. The walk might help to clear the confusion from his brain; at any rate, it was out of the question to follow Mrs. Tracy home. First of all he had to be by himself, and in the open.вБ† вБ†вА¶

The Eaglewood gate was unlatched, and he walked along the drive to the house. It lay low and spreading under mysterious tree shadows, and he thought of the day when he had been caught asleep in the broken-down motor, and brought there, a bewildered culprit, by the scandalized Jacob. Every step was thick with memories which in the making had worn no special look of happiness, but were now steeped in it. He remembered how ashamed and angry he had been when Jacob had shoved him into the hall among those unknown people.вБ† вБ†вА¶

Through the canopy of foliage one or two lights peeped from the upper panes, but the lower floor was dark, the shutters were not closed. Perhaps everybody was still out. Silence and night seemed stealing unnoticed into the empty rooms, and Vance would have liked to open one of the windows and enter too.вБ† вБ†вА¶ But as he stood on the lawn, a little way off, a light appeared in one of the drawing room windows, and he heard a few chords struck on the piano, and voices rising and falling. Without looking to see who was in the room he hurried back into the shade of the drive, and dropped the keys into the letter box by the front door. They were all strangers to him, those peopleвБ†вАФhe did not belong up there, in the light and music and ease, any more than he did in the dismal Tracy house below. He walked away heavy with that sense of inexorable solitude which sometimes oppresses young hearts. From all the glorious worlds he could imagine he seemed equally shut out.вБ† вБ†вА¶

Where did he belong then? Why, with himself! The idea came like a flash of light. What did he want with other peopleвАЩs worlds when he would create universes of his own? What he wanted was independence, freedom, solitudeвБ†вАФand they cost less than houses and furniture, and much less than human ties. Mrs. TracyвАЩs words came back to him: вАЬThink what it would be to you to be free yourself.вАЭ

Out in the lane again, he continued to climb. Through pearly clouds the moon of late summer climbed with him, lighting the way capriciously, as it had been lit by HaloвАЩs motor lamps that morning before daylight when they had mounted to Thundertop. He had been free then, the world had lain before him in all its conquerable glory. And a few months of discouragement and the unexpected sight of a fresh face had annihilated everything, and reduced him to the poor thing he now was. He walked and walked, unconscious of the way, driven by the need of being alone and far from the realities to which he must so soon come back. At length he threw himself down on a ledge from which he could catch a first twist of the moon-silvered Hudson, remote and embosomed in midnight forest.
вАЬThink what it would be to you to be free.вАЭ That was what they were offering himвБ† вБ†вА¶ the return to that other world, the world he had looked down on from Thundertop.

He tried to clear his mind of anger and confusion and look at his case dispassionately. The Tracy case was plain enough; from the first, very likely, Mrs. Tracy had been bent on getting rid of him, on giving Laura Lou what she called her chance. But she was not a woman of initiative, and would probably have ended by resigning herself if some persistent influence had not pushed her forward. UptonвАЩsвБ†вАФ? Vance shrugged away the thought of UptonвБ† вБ†вА¶ Hayes, of course! The blood rushed to his forehead. He could hear Bunty Hayes rehearsing the scene with Mrs. Tracy. Very likely they had cooked up the anonymous letter between them. And now they were going to turn him out like a boarder whose room was wantedвБ† вБ†вА¶ that was about the way they looked at it.вБ† вБ†вА¶ вАЬNothing easierвБ† вБ†вА¶ slick as a buttonвБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ Hayes would say.вБ† вБ†вА¶
Well, why not?

He lay there, stretched out on the ledge, and looked down into the nocturnal depths. In the contemplation of that widespread beauty calmer thoughts came back, little things fell away.вБ† вБ†вА¶ What had he been able to do for Laura Lou? What had she done for him? The tie between them had so quickly become a habit, an aquiescence, nothing more. Perhaps Laura Lou had been conscious of that too, perhaps she too had chafed and longed for freedomвБ†вАФor simply for relief from worry, for material well-being, a home of her ownвБ†вАФall that he could never give her. His eyes filled.вБ† вБ†вА¶ What was there to prevent their both trying again?

He could not find the answer to this question. He had never heard of any reason why married people shouldnвАЩt agree to part and begin life again if they wanted toвБ† вБ†вА¶ only he could not picture its happening to himself and Laura Lou. Whenever he tried to, it was as if a million delicate tendrils, of which he was unconscious when he and she were together, tightened about his heart and held it fast to hers, in a strange bondage closer than that of love or desire. He lay and lay there, and tried to puzzle it out, but in vain. His last conclusion was: вАЬOf course if she really wants to be free, she shall beвБ†вАФвАЭ and for an instant his soul blazed again with the rekindled light of opportunity.вБ† вБ†вА¶ After that, worn out, he fell asleep on his ledge.вБ† вБ†вА¶

It was broad daylight when he woke up, aching and hungry. His watch had stopped, but from the position of the sun he judged it must be after eight oвАЩclock. His first craving was that of a ravenous boyвБ†вАФfor a cup of Mrs. TracyвАЩs hot coffee. What was all the fuss about, anyhow? How different things looked by daylight, with the same old sun lighting up the same old daily road of duty. There was the office, there was his wifeвБ†вАФand, oh hallelujah, there was his work! He sprang down from rock to rock, reached the road, and hurried homeward.

When he got to Mrs. TracyвАЩs door he ran up the steps calling out Laura LouвАЩs name. What nonsense it all seemed now, that ranting scene of last night! But to his astonishment the door was locked. He called again, shook the handle, banged on the panelвБ†вАФbut no one answered. He walked around to the back of the house, expecting to find his mother-in-law among the currant bushes. вАЬLaura Lou is sound asleep still, I suppose?вАЭ he would begin jokingly. But there was no one among the vegetables either, and the door of the back porch was also locked. Vance stepped back and threw a handful of pebbles at his wifeвАЩs window, calling out to her again. Again there was no answer.
He returned to the front door, shook and rattled it without result, and finally, disheartened, sat down on the doorstep and wondered.вБ† вБ†вА¶

All the way down the mountain conciliatory phrases, jokes, words of endearment had crowded to his lipsвБ†вАФanything to smooth the way back to the old life. And now he was confronted by this silent hostile house, which seemed to know nothing of him, but stared down from blank forbidding windows as if on an intruder.

An intruder? Was it possible that he was already that? By God, no! His smouldering wrath against Mrs. Tracy blazed up again. It was all her doing, whatever had happened. She wouldnвАЩt rest till she had separated him from Laura Lou.вБ† вБ†вА¶ He was beginning to feel a sort of terror at the silent house and the unknown behind it. He saw the son of a neighbour walking down the lane, and stood up and hailed him. вАЬSee hereвБ†вАФIвАЩm just home, and I guess IвАЩm locked out. Seen my womenfolk round anywhere?вАЭ he asked facetiously.

Yes, the youth said, he had; heвАЩd met them up the lane. They were going to see if they could get DixonвАЩs team to take them to the station, they told him; he guessed they were taking some baggage with them, for a stay.вБ† вБ†вА¶ He grinned and loafed on, and Vance stood and looked after him without moving. It had all been planned in advance, then; they were going to the cityвБ†вАФgoing straight to Bunty Hayes, no doubt! He would have secured rooms for them, where they could stay till they started for California.вБ† вБ†вА¶

Vance walked dizzily back to the porch. It was true, thenвБ†вАФit had really happened, this thing which was still inconceivable to him. He stood there staring about him like a man waking out of sleep; and as he did so, he noticed a suitcase and two or three insecurely tied bundles in a corner of the porch. They had already packed, then; they would come back presently to pick up their belongings.

It was all as queer and telescopic as things piled up in a nightmare. Yesterday morning this house had been his home and Laura LouвАЩsвБ†вАФnow it was shut, empty, unrelated to them. That phase of its existence and theirs was over.вБ† вБ†вА¶ His eye lit casually on the suitcase, and he recognized it as the one which had accompanied him and Laura Lou on their honeymoon. He sat down beside it on the floor of the porch, and recalled how he had unpacked it on the beach on their wedding day and pulled out their sticky oozing wedding breakfast. And how Laura Lou had had her first taste of champagne out of a seashell! And it had been his first taste, tooвБ†вАФonly he had got out of the way remembering that.вБ† вБ†вА¶

His eyes filled, and he was overcome by the sudden boyish craving to see something belonging to her and touch it. It seemed to him now that he and she had really parted long ago; that the laughing child who had helped him to unpack the lunch from that suitcase had for months past been farther from him than there were miles between PaulвАЩs Landing and California. He had not meant it to be so; had not been aware that it was soвБ†вАФbut now it came over him that perhaps, for Laura Lou, the knot had long since been untied, that the anonymous letter had been a pretext long waited for, perhaps provoked. At the thought, the physical sense of her stole back on him; it was clinging and potent, like the perfume of a garden in June. Vance bent over the suitcase, and hardly knowing what he did, pushed open the lock and lifted the lid. Her poor little possessions had been crammed in carelessly and in haste, and on top, flattened out and disjointed, lay the old stuffed dove from the gilt basket he had sent to her mother.

The sight caught him by the throat. He knelt for a long time, clutching the limp moth-eaten bird in both hands. He had not thought of the dove for agesвБ†вАФbut he remembered now having noticed that she had fastened it by a wire above the little looking glass on her chest of drawers. It had hung there, a little crooked, with one wing limp, ever since they had come back from their honeymoon.

He had hardly noticed it; but she had remembered, in the haste and grief of her going, to unfasten it from its perch and cram it into her bursting suitcase. вАЬBut if she feels like that, why is she going?вАЭ The thought rushed through him like a burst of warm rain in spring, softening, vivifying. He was afraid of nothing now, with the old stuffed dove in his hand! He stood up, pressing it to him, as the wheels of DixonвАЩs carryall halted before the gate.
